Name: kv_cache_optimization
Title: Efficient KV-Cache Management for Long-Context LLM Inference via Hardware-Aware Kernel Fusion
Short Hypothesis: By fusing KV-cache updates with attention computations in CUDA/Triton kernels and optimizing memory-bandwidth utilization, we can reduce latency and improve throughput for long-context LLM inference on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Existing KV-cache optimizations focus on compression or sparsity (e.g., FlashAttention, Sparse Attention), but these often introduce overhead or fail to fully exploit hardware capabilities. Our approach differs by directly fusing KV-cache updates with attention kernels, minimizing memory round-trips and leveraging tensor-core occupancy.
Abstract: Long-context LLM inference faces significant bottlenecks due to inefficient KV-cache management, particularly in memory-bound operations. We propose a novel hardware-aware optimization that fuses KV-cache updates with attention computations in CUDA/Triton kernels. By reducing memory round-trips and improving tensor-core utilization, our method achieves measurable speedups in latency and throughput for long-context inference on commodity GPUs. We validate our approach through detailed profiling, including warp stall analysis, achieved bandwidth, and arithmetic intensity, demonstrating end-to-end improvements in p50/p99 latency and tokens-per-second metrics.
Experiments: Implement fused KV-cache update and attention kernels in CUDA/Triton.
Profile baseline and optimized kernels using Nsight Compute, measuring warp stalls, HBM utilization, and arithmetic intensity.
Benchmark p50/p99 latency and tokens-per-second on a commodity GPU (e.g., NVIDIA A100) with long-context inputs (e.g., 8K tokens).
Compare energy per token and throughput under batching scenarios.
Validate robustness across multiple GPUs and driver versions, reporting variance as measured jitter.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Fusion may increase register pressure, limiting occupancy.
Optimizations may not generalize to all GPU architectures.
Long-context inputs may expose edge cases in kernel design.
Profiling overhead could obscure real-world benefits.
